fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This is the CORE of your portfolio — a low-cost index fund (only 0.20% yearly fee) that tracks India's top 150 mid-sized companies. Over 20 years, keeping fees tiny makes a massive difference to your final corpus. It has delivered 18.7% yearly over 5 years while costing almost nothing. This anchors the portfolio with broad mid-cap exposure without relying on any single fund manager's calls.

fund.name: Nippon India Nifty Smallcap 250 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Your satellite small-cap INDEX position — the broad basket of India's 250 smallest listed companies at just 0.20% yearly fee. Since you prefer 'index + small-cap satellite' and are comfortable with high equity, this gives you cheap, diversified exposure to India's growth engine. Small caps can drop 40%+ in bad years but over a 20-year window they have historically delivered the highest wealth creation of any category.

fund.name: BANDHAN SMALL CAP FUND - DIRECT PLAN GROWTH

allocations.rationale: Your ACTIVE small-cap satellite — the best performing small-cap active fund over 5 years (23.7% CAGR) with a stunning 31.3% 3-year return. Paired alongside the index small-cap fund, this gives you both cheap passive coverage and an active manager who has genuinely beaten the index. Bandhan's small-cap team has shown real stock-picking ability. A large fund (₹8,900 Cr) with stable management. Warning: this can drop 40%+ in a bad year — that is normal and expected.

fund.name: HDFC Mid Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: India's largest mid-cap active fund (₹24,347 Cr) with a remarkable 18-year track record and 21.8% over 5 years. HDFC's mid-cap team has consistently identified growing companies before they become large-caps — exactly the Peter Lynch 'find them before the crowd' approach. The fund runs with slightly lower risk than its peers (beta 0.89) while still delivering superior returns. One of the most battle-tested mid-cap funds in India — it has survived 2008, 2011, 2015, 2018, and 2020 market crashes and come out ahead.

fund.name: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: This is your PORTFOLIO SHOCK-ABSORBER. Parag Parikh invests ~25% overseas (US tech giants like Alphabet, Meta, Microsoft) and the rest in Indian stocks with a deep-value, buy-and-hold approach. When Indian markets crash, this fund tends to hold up far better — it fell only half as much as other equity funds in 2022's global selloff. For a sailor who's at sea and can't react quickly to market turbulence, having this 'calm during storms' fund is invaluable. India's largest fund by AUM (₹88,400 Cr) — rock-solid institution.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: A small but powerful global diversifier — tracks the top 500 US companies (Apple, Nvidia, Amazon, etc.) at just 0.20% yearly fee. Over 3 years it has returned 23.8% CAGR. When the Indian Rupee weakens (which historically happens over long periods), this fund gains extra returns in ₹ terms. Daughter's education abroad? US-dollar-linked returns in your portfolio act as a natural hedge for that goal. Keep this to 5% — it has its own risks (US market valuations, currency, RBI/SEBI overseas investment limits).

plan.setup_phase:
**Step-by-step: Getting started (even from ship)**

**Step 1 — Open a Direct Mutual Fund Account**
Use Kuvera (kuvera.in) or MFU (mfuonline.com) — both work entirely online. You need your PAN, Aadhaar, and a bank account. KYC (identity check) takes 10-15 minutes with a selfie and Aadhaar OTP. You can do this from anywhere in the world with a decent internet connection.

**Step 2 — Link Your Bank Account**
Link your primary savings account (ideally HDFC/ICICI/SBI for instant UPI mandates). Set up an auto-debit mandate (called e-NACH) for your SIPs — this means ₹57,000/month will be automatically invested even when you're at sea.

**Step 3 — Deploy the Existing ₹20 Lakh Corpus**
DO NOT invest all ₹20 lakhs in one shot. Instead, invest it in two tranches:
- Tranche 1 (Now): ₹10 lakhs split across the 5 funds as per the lumpsum amounts above. This gets you immediately invested.
- Tranche 2 (3-6 months later or on your next shore leave): ₹10 lakhs in the same ratio. This reduces the risk of investing all your money at a market peak.

**Step 4 — Start Your SIPs**
Set up monthly SIPs totalling ₹57,000/month (₹3,000 spare buffer kept back). SIP dates: 5th of every month. Even when you're at sea — the auto-debit will handle it.

**Step 5 — Lumpsum on Shore Leave**
Each time you return home (every ~4 months), invest any additional saved surplus as a lumpsum in the same fund ratio. This replicates your natural investment pattern — big chunks when ashore, steady SIP in between.

**Step 6 — Nominee and Contact**
Add a nominee (your daughter/spouse) to all folios. Keep your registered mobile number active even at sea (use a roaming SIM or ensure a family member can check your email for any OTPs).
review_checkpoints: **Every shore leave (~4 months): Quick 30-minute check.** Log into Kuvera. Confirm all 5 SIPs are running without failures. Check if any fund has been consistently delivering far less than its peers for 2+ quarters in a row. If yes, flag it but don't panic — don't exit just because of one bad quarter. Deploy any fresh lumpsum savings in the same fund ratio.

**Once a year (ideally Dec-Jan): Annual portfolio health check (1-2 hours).** Review whether your total portfolio is still split roughly 65% mid/small cap growth + 20% quality active + 10% global + 5% flexible. If small caps have boomed and now represent 40%+ of your total wealth, trim slightly and rebalance to target. Check if any fund has changed its fund manager — manager continuity matters a lot in active funds like HDFC Mid Cap.

**At 5 years (Captain Haddock age ~45): First major milestone check.** You should have a corpus of ₹60-80 lakhs (base scenario). Review whether you're on track for the ₹8 Cr goal. If markets have given unusually high returns (18%+), consider locking in some profits by adding a debt/hybrid allocation of 10-15% for stability.

**At 10 years (~age 50): Mid-journey review.** Daughter's education may be 6-8 years away. Start moving 15-20% of the total corpus into safer options (a short-duration debt fund) to protect the education fund portion from a market crash just before you need it.

**At 15 years (~age 55): Pre-retirement positioning.** Begin gradually reducing small-cap weight from 40% to 20%. Shift freed-up money into Parag Parikh or an Aggressive Hybrid. Goal: reduce the chance of a 40% market crash wiping out 5 years of gains just before retirement.

**At 18 years (~age 58): Final lap.** Start a Systematic Withdrawal Plan (SWP) design for your retirement income. Move 30% of corpus to debt/hybrid funds. Keep 70% in equity for continued long-term growth post-retirement.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines:
**Once a year — during your shore leave — do a simple rebalancing check:**

Your target split is:
- Index funds (Midcap 150 + Smallcap 250): 45%
- Active equity (Bandhan Small Cap + HDFC Mid Cap): 40%
- Global/Flexi stabiliser (Parag Parikh + S&P 500): 15%

**When to rebalance:** If any single category drifts more than 10 percentage points from target (e.g., small caps become 55% of your portfolio after a rally), sell the excess and top up the laggards. Do this ONCE a year — not more.

**How to rebalance without selling:** Prefer redirecting new SIP/lumpsum money to underweight funds rather than selling — this avoids triggering tax on gains. Selling means paying 12.5% tax on profits above ₹1.25 lakhs/year.

**Tax-smart tip:** You are in the new regime, so you don't get 80C benefits. However, all 6 funds are equity-taxed (LTCG at 12.5% on gains above ₹1.25L held >1 year). Always hold for more than 1 year before any redemption to get the lower 12.5% tax rate instead of the 20% short-term rate. Use your ₹1.25 lakh per year LTCG exemption — book gains up to that amount each year tax-free (called 'tax harvesting') and reinvest immediately.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
**passive_instrument_fraction (0.50):** The equity sleeve contains 6 funds totalling 100% of the allocation. Of these, 3 are passive/index funds: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index (25%), Nippon India Nifty Smallcap 250 Index (20%), and Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index (5%) — totalling 50% passive equity. The remaining 50% is in active funds: Bandhan Small Cap (20%), HDFC Mid Cap (20%), and Parag Parikh Flexi Cap (10%). Exactly half passive, half active → 0.50.

**turnover_score (0.75):** The plan strongly favours a buy-and-hold approach. Rebalancing is recommended once a year at most, preferably via redirecting new money rather than selling. The review checkpoints are annual or multi-year. The explicit caution "do this ONCE a year — not more" and the tax-smart tip to avoid selling all reinforce low turnover. Minor deductions for the tactical shore-leave reviews and the conditional rebalancing trigger (10pp drift rule). → 0.75.

**cost_emphasis_score (0.65):** Cost minimisation features prominently — the rationale for every index fund explicitly highlights the "0.20% yearly fee" and contrasts it with active fund costs. The rebalancing guidelines also cite the 12.5% LTCG tax and recommend redirecting SIPs to avoid selling (tax efficiency). However, the plan also includes active funds chosen for performance track records, so costs are not the sole dominant criterion. → 0.65.

**research_vs_cost_score (0.40):** The plan does engage in meaningful fund-specific research: it references HDFC Mid Cap's 18-year track record, manager continuity, beta of 0.89, Bandhan's stock-picking ability, Parag Parikh's overseas allocation and defensive characteristics, and specific alpha/return data for each fund. This is more than pure index-following but less than deep stock-level analysis (no PEG ratios or company moat analysis). Index funds are included precisely to avoid research dependency. Balanced tilt toward research side. → 0.40.

**time_horizon_alignment_score (0.85):** The plan is explicitly structured around a 20-year investment horizon with milestone checkpoints at 5, 10, 15, and 18 years. SIPs are automated to continue even when the investor is at sea. The rationale emphasises long-term compounding, holding through crashes (2008, 2011, etc.), and avoiding panic exits. Only minor deductions for the shore-leave tactical lumpsum deployment and the drift-based rebalancing trigger. → 0.85.

**portfolio_activeness_score (0.35):** Assessing the weighted risk metrics across all funds: the three index funds (50% weight combined) show expected passive behaviour (beta ~1.0 for mid/small-cap indices vs Nifty 50 proxy, though tracking errors are elevated due to the mid/small-cap vs large-cap comparison). The active funds show genuinely active characteristics — HDFC Mid Cap has alpha of 14.48 and tracking error of 7.83; Bandhan Small Cap has alpha 9.78 and TE 11.72; Parag Parikh has alpha 11.63 and TE 9.35 but low beta (0.55) due to global exposure. The S&P 500 fund shows extreme metrics (alpha 49, TE 21.39) because it's being measured against Nifty 50 — this is an artefact of the benchmark mismatch, not genuine active management. Overall, the portfolio blends genuine index exposure with high-alpha active funds in roughly equal measure, with the active funds showing meaningful tracking error and alpha. Slightly below mid-point toward active side. → 0.35.

pqs.reasoning:
**Goal Alignment (0.88):** The plan is strongly aligned with Captain Haddock's profile. The 20-year aggressive equity tilt (100% equity, heavy mid/small cap) suits the retirement corpus goal of ₹8 Cr. The lump sum + SIP hybrid approach directly mirrors the sailor's natural cash flow pattern. The S&P 500 fund is explicitly linked to daughter's education abroad (USD hedge). The glide-path in review checkpoints (gradually de-risking at ages 50, 55, 58) is thoughtfully tied to goals. Minor gaps: no explicit sub-corpus earmarked for the house-building goal, and SIP total (₹57,000) leaves only a ₹3,000 buffer from the ₹60,000 surplus without explanation of why not fully deployed. The lumpsum deployment of ₹20L is split into tranches of ₹10L each — but the plan's allocations only explicitly list ₹20L lumpsum total (₹500k+₹400k+₹400k+₹400k+₹200k+₹100k = ₹20L), which is consistent.

**Diversification (0.75):** The plan achieves reasonable diversification across market caps (mid, small, flexi), fund houses (Motilal, Nippon, Bandhan, HDFC, PPFAS), and geographies (India + US via PPFAS and S&P 500). However, the plan is entirely equity — there is zero allocation to debt, gold, or hybrid instruments at the outset. While the investor is aggressive and the horizon is 20 years, a 100% equity portfolio with ~40% in mid/small cap index funds and another 40% in active mid/small caps creates significant concentration in the mid/small-cap segment (80% combined). Large-cap and large-cap index exposure is entirely absent (Nifty 50, for instance), which is a notable gap for a core holding. The global allocation is minimal at 15% (PPFAS + S&P 500). The plan does address adding debt at the 5-year checkpoint, but the starting allocation is undiversified by asset class.

**Risk-Return Appropriateness (0.82):** The aggressive risk profile is well-served. Projected returns (base 14.5%, bull 18.5%, bear 10.5%) are realistic for a mid/small cap heavy portfolio over 20 years. The plan explicitly warns of 40%+ drawdowns in small caps and notes this is normal — appropriate for the investor's stated comfort. The lumpsum tranching strategy mitigates timing risk. The use of PPFAS as a shock-absorber is well-reasoned for an investor who is at sea and can't react quickly. The 5% S&P 500 allocation is modest and appropriate. The concern is that 80% mid/small exposure is very aggressive even for an aggressive investor at age 40 with no large-cap anchor, but this is not wholly inappropriate given the 20-year horizon and stated preferences. No SIP step-up is planned despite the investor likely receiving salary increments — a missed opportunity.

**Internal Consistency (0.80):** The plan is largely coherent. The stated target split in rebalancing guidelines (Index 45% + Active equity 40% + Global/Flexi 15%) matches the allocation percentages. The rationale for each fund aligns with the overall strategy narrative. The setup phase logically addresses the at-sea constraint. However, there is a minor inconsistency: the rebalancing section describes a 5-fund split but the plan has 6 funds — the S&P 500 is listed as lumpsum-only but appears in the rebalancing framework under "Global/Flexi." The SIP total (₹57,000) doesn't include the S&P 500 (which has no SIP), consistent with lumpsum-only mode. The rationale field is empty, and the risks field is empty — these are structural gaps, though individual fund rationales and review checkpoints partially compensate. The "HDFC Mid Cap" is called "active mid-cap" yet classified under an "Index + small-cap satellite" preferred investor — this dual active/index approach is somewhat at odds with stated preferences but is explained as a complementary pairing.

**Tax Efficiency (0.82):** The plan performs well on tax reasoning. It explicitly identifies that the investor is in the new regime (no 80C benefit, hence no ELSS recommended — correct). It correctly states all 6 funds are equity-taxed (LTCG 12.5% above ₹1.25L, STCG 20%), which is accurate for equity/flexi/index funds with ≥65% equity. The plan introduces tax harvesting (booking up to ₹1.25L gains annually) — a sophisticated and actionable recommendation. The rebalancing guideline to redirect fresh money rather than sell is tax-smart. The plan correctly discourages short-term redemptions. Minor gaps: the plan doesn't explicitly address the tax treatment of the S&P 500 fund — as an overseas fund-of-fund, it may be taxed as a debt fund (slab rate) depending on its structure, which would be a material tax consideration. This is the main tax blind spot in an otherwise solid tax section.
